skill.name: swarm-orchestration
skill.description: Orchestrate multi-agent swarms with agentic-flow for parallel task execution, dynamic topology, and intelligent coordination. Use when scaling beyond single agents, implementing complex workflows, or building distributed AI systems.
skill.summary: Coordinate multi-agent workflows with agentic-flow swarm patterns, hooks, memory, and topology guidance.
skill.author: ruvnet

security_audit.summary: Static external command findings are true positives for the Bash examples that run npx agentic-flow hooks. The weak cryptography and reconnaissance findings appear to be false positives from ordinary prose and task labels, with no evidence of malicious intent or prompt injection.

medium_findings.title: External CLI Execution Guidance
medium_findings.description: The skill instructs users to run npx agentic-flow hook commands. This is legitimate for an orchestration skill, but it executes external package code and can affect local project state or swarm sessions.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: The Bash examples directly invoke npx agentic-flow commands. The surrounding context is normal tool usage, so the risk is real but not clearly malicious.

low_findings.title: Shared Memory Requires Data Hygiene
low_findings.description: The skill demonstrates storing and retrieving shared swarm memory. This can spread sensitive project context across agents if users store secrets or private data without review.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The memory example is generic and not malicious. It is still a plausible privacy risk in multi-agent workflows.

low_findings.title: Weak Cryptography Findings Dismissed
low_findings.description: The static weak-cryptography matches are false positives. The cited lines contain prose or task labels, not cryptographic algorithms, hashing APIs, or security-sensitive code.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: Manual review found no cryptographic operation at the cited locations. The matches appear to come from normal words inside documentation examples.

low_findings.title: Reconnaissance Findings Dismissed
low_findings.description: The static reconnaissance matches are false positives. The cited hook examples use task and session identifiers, not host enumeration, network discovery, or system inventory commands.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The reviewed lines only show agentic-flow hook arguments. No reconnaissance command or collection behavior is present.

dangerous_patterns.title: npx-Based Command Execution
dangerous_patterns.description: The documentation includes npx commands that may download or execute external CLI code. Users should pin trusted package versions and run commands in controlled workspaces.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: The shell examples are explicit and user-facing. They are expected for this tool, but they remain an external command execution pattern.

content.value_statement: Complex AI workflows can stall when one agent handles every task alone. This skill shows how to coordinate agentic-flow swarms with topology choices, hooks, memory, and execution patterns.
actual_capabilities: Shows how to initialize mesh, hierarchical, and adaptive swarm topologies.
Provides examples for spawning coder, tester, reviewer, and architect agents.
Documents parallel, pipeline, and adaptive task execution patterns.
Explains shared memory coordination for agent context handoff.
Covers load balancing, resiliency, metrics, and hook-based synchronization.
limitations: Requires the external agentic-flow CLI and compatible runtime setup.
Does not implement the swarm engine inside the skill itself.
Examples need project-specific validation before production use.
External commands should be run only in trusted workspaces.

use_cases.title: Parallel Feature Delivery
use_cases.description: Split implementation, testing, review, and frontend work across specialized agents for one coordinated delivery cycle.

use_cases.title: AI Workflow Prototyping
use_cases.description: Compare mesh, hierarchical, and adaptive coordination patterns before building a larger agent workflow.

use_cases.title: Coordinated Automation Runs
use_cases.description: Use hooks and shared memory to keep long-running automation tasks synchronized across agents and sessions.

prompt_templates.title: Start a Small Swarm
prompt_templates.scenario: Beginner setup for a simple coordinated task.
prompt_templates.prompt: Use the swarm-orchestration skill to design a three-agent workflow for this task: [task]. Include topology, agent roles, and safe setup steps.

prompt_templates.title: Choose a Topology
prompt_templates.scenario: Select the best coordination model for a project.
prompt_templates.prompt: Compare mesh, hierarchical, and adaptive swarm topologies for [project]. Recommend one option and explain the tradeoffs.

prompt_templates.title: Plan Parallel Execution
prompt_templates.scenario: Break a complex delivery into agent assignments.
prompt_templates.prompt: Create a parallel swarm execution plan for [goal]. Define agents, dependencies, shared memory keys, timeouts, and review checkpoints.

prompt_templates.title: Audit a Swarm Workflow
prompt_templates.scenario: Advanced review of reliability and operational risk.
prompt_templates.prompt: Review this swarm workflow for coordination failures, unsafe command usage, secret exposure, and missing recovery steps: [workflow].

output_examples.input: Plan a swarm to build and test a REST API.
output: Recommended topology: hierarchical, with an architect coordinating coder, tester, and reviewer agents.
Execution flow: design first, implementation second, tests third, review last.
Safety notes: verify CLI package source, set timeouts, and avoid storing secrets in shared memory.

output_examples.input: Choose a topology for a research-heavy automation task.
output: Recommended topology: mesh, because research agents can compare findings in parallel.
Use shared memory for source lists, assumptions, and final synthesis notes.
Add a reviewer agent to resolve conflicting conclusions before final output.

output_examples.input: Improve reliability for a long-running swarm.
output: Enable retry limits, backoff, and task reassignment for failed agents.
Track throughput, latency, success rate, and agent utilization.
Use post-task hooks to synchronize final state before ending the session.

best_practices: Pin and verify external CLI dependencies before running npx commands.
Start with a small agent count, then expand after observing coordination quality.
Keep secrets out of shared memory and task descriptions.
anti_patterns: Running swarm commands in sensitive repositories without reviewing package provenance.
Assigning overlapping responsibilities to agents without clear ownership.
Using adaptive execution without timeouts, review checkpoints, or recovery steps.

faq.question: What does this skill help coordinate?
faq.answer: It helps plan agentic-flow swarms for parallel tasks, pipeline workflows, memory sharing, and hook-based synchronization.

faq.question: Does the skill run commands by itself?
faq.answer: No. It documents commands and patterns that a user may choose to run in a prepared environment.

faq.question: Which tools can use this skill?
faq.answer: The report lists support for Claude, Codex, and Claude Code.

faq.question: Why is the security risk medium?
faq.answer: The skill includes npx command examples that execute external CLI code, even though the intent appears legitimate.

faq.question: Can it manage production swarms automatically?
faq.answer: It provides orchestration guidance, but production use still needs validation, monitoring, access controls, and dependency review.

faq.question: How should shared memory be used safely?
faq.answer: Store only necessary task context, avoid secrets, and review what each agent can read before starting the workflow.
